original_abstract: Porous materials are essential for industrial gas separations, offering a unique platform where adsorbent properties can be precisely engineered to target specific molecules. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as a versatile class of porous solids, providing nearly endless opportunities to design materials with tailored functionalities. Because the structural possibilities are so vast, quantum mechanical computations have become an indispensable tool for prioritizing the synthesis of the most promising candidates. In this study, we employ electronic structure calculations to predict a new metal-organic framework specifically designed for the separation of dinitrogen from methane—a technically demanding process that is vital for the purification and utilization of natural gas. Our results indicate that incorporating open V(II) sites into a MOF creates an environment where dinitrogen is adsorbed with a significantly higher enthalpy than methane. This high selectivity is driven by strong π-backbonding between the vanadium center and dinitrogen, an interaction that is fundamentally absent for methane.
humanized_abstract: Porous materials are widely used in industrial gas separations, where adsorbent properties can be tailored towards particular gas molecules. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as a class of porous solids provide nearly limitless versatility for designing materials with specific functionalities. Given the numerous structural possibilities of MOFs, quantum mechanical calculations can serve as a valuable aid in deciding which structures to prepare. We employ electronic structure calculations to demonstrate a MOF that can be synthesized for separation of dinitrogen from methane—a very challenging process, however crucial for purification and utilization of natural gas. The calculated adsorption enthalpies for the new metal-organic framework show that open V(II) sites within the framework can selectively adsorb dinitrogen with a higher enthalpy than methane. Higher adsorption enthalpy for dinitrogen over methane is attributed to stronger π-backbonding occurring between V and dinitrogen which is lacking between V and methane.

The calculated adsorption enthalpies for the new metal-organic framework show that open V(II) sites within the framework can selectively adsorb dinitrogen with a higher enthalpy than methane. Higher adsorption enthalpy for dinitrogen over methane is attributed to stronger π-backbonding occurring between V and dinitrogen which is lacking between V and methane.
